title: Topic 116: Influencer Marketing and Game Spending: When Your Favorite Streamer Sells Out
content:
# Topic 116: Influencer Marketing and Game Spending: When Your Favorite Streamer Sells Out

## Abstract

This paper delves into the peculiar and often bewildering world of influencer marketing within the video game industry, a realm where authenticity and commercialism engage in a perpetual, often clumsy, dance. We explore the psychological underpinnings of why audiences are so susceptible to the endorsements of their favorite streamers, examining the potent cocktail of parasocial relationships, social proof, and the carefully constructed illusion of friendship. Through a series of case studies, from the stratospheric rise of games like *Among Us* to the spectacular implosion of high-profile exclusivity deals, we dissect the financial and cultural impact of streamers “selling out.” This paper argues that while influencer marketing can be a powerful tool for game developers, its effectiveness is precariously balanced on a tightrope of perceived authenticity, and the fall from grace can be as swift as a rage-quit in a competitive shooter. We conclude with a sobering, yet humorous, reflection on what this phenomenon reveals about modern consumer behavior and the ever-blurring lines between entertainment, friendship, and commerce.

## Introduction

In the grand, pixelated theater of the internet, a new kind of celebrity has emerged: the video game streamer. These digital jesters, part-time comedians, and full-time button-mashers have captured the hearts and wallets of millions. They are the new kings and queens of the digital age, their thrones built upon a mountain of discarded energy drink cans and their scepters glowing with the RGB lighting of a high-end gaming mouse. But with great power comes great responsibility, and an even greater opportunity to sell stuff. This paper, therefore, embarks on a perilous quest to understand the phenomenon of influencer marketing in gaming, or as it’s more colloquially known, “when your favorite streamer sells out.”

We’ve all been there. You’re watching your favorite streamer, the one you’ve followed for years, the one who feels like a genuine friend, and then it happens. They start gushing about a new mobile game with all the subtlety of a sledgehammer. Suddenly, their once-sacred stream is plastered with more logos than a NASCAR driver’s jumpsuit. The betrayal is palpable. It’s like finding out your childhood imaginary friend is now a paid spokesperson for a brand of gluten-free, artisanal, small-batch potato chips. This paper will explore the emotional and psychological rollercoaster that is influencer marketing in gaming, from the initial sting of betrayal to the grudging acceptance that, yes, even our digital heroes have bills to pay. We will examine the delicate dance between authenticity and advertising, and how the slightest misstep can send a streamer tumbling from the pedestal of public adoration into the murky swamp of sell-out-dom. So, grab your popcorn (and your wallet, because you’re probably about to be influenced), and let’s dive into the hilarious, and often cringeworthy, world of influencer marketing in gaming.

## Literature Review

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not been entirely ignorant of the burgeoning field of influencer marketing. Scholars, in their tweed jackets and ivory towers, have peered down at the digital playground of Twitch and YouTube and offered their two cents, albeit in a language that often requires a Ph.D. to decipher. This section will attempt to translate their findings into something resembling plain English, with a healthy dose of comedic commentary.

A significant body of research has focused on the psychological mechanisms that make influencer marketing so darn effective. The concept of **parasocial relationships**, first coined in the 1950s to describe the one-sided relationships television viewers formed with on-screen personalities, has found a new and even more potent home in the world of live streaming [1]. Unlike the distant, pre-recorded celebrities of yesteryear, today’s streamers offer a constant, seemingly unfiltered window into their lives. They stream for hours on end, sharing personal stories, inside jokes, and even their moments of vulnerability. This constant exposure, as one study from a prominent health and gaming organization points out, can trick our primitive, monkey brains into believing we have a genuine, reciprocal friendship with these digital entertainers [2]. Our brains, it seems, haven’t quite caught up to the fact that our “friend” is also a “friend” to 10,000 other people in the chat.

Building on this foundation of faux-friendship, researchers have also highlighted the role of **social proof** and **authenticity**. When a streamer we trust and admire endorses a product, our brains, in a desperate attempt to simplify the complex world of consumer choice, take a shortcut. “If my favorite streamer likes it,” our subconscious whispers, “then it must be good.” This is social proof in action, the same reason we’re more likely to eat at a restaurant with a long line out the door (or, in the digital age, a product with thousands of positive reviews). However, this only works if the streamer is perceived as authentic. The moment an endorsement feels forced, disingenuous, or, heaven forbid, like a blatant cash grab, the illusion shatters. The streamer is no longer a trusted friend, but a digital snake oil salesman, and the backlash can be swift and merciless. As one industry analysis puts it, “Trust and credibility are essential components of successful influencer marketing campaigns” [3]. No kidding.

Finally, we have the more recent, and dare we say, more cynical, research into the **economics of influencer marketing**. Studies have attempted to quantify the return on investment (ROI) of sponsored streams, with some rather surprising results. One study, for instance, found that for most games, sponsored streams are a money-loser, with a median ROI of -95% [4]. This suggests that while influencer marketing can generate hype and awareness, it doesn’t always translate into actual sales. It seems that even when we’re being influenced, we’re not always influenced enough to actually open our wallets. This has led to a more nuanced understanding of influencer marketing, one that acknowledges its role in brand building and community engagement, rather than just direct sales. It’s not just about selling a product; it’s about selling a feeling, a sense of belonging, and the vague, aspirational promise that if you buy this game, you’ll be just as cool, funny, and beloved as your favorite streamer. And who can put a price on that? (Apparently, about negative 95 percent).

## Analysis

So, what’s really going on in our brains when we see our favorite streamer suddenly hawking a new game? Are we just mindless drones, ready to throw our money at whatever shiny new object is dangled in front of us? Well, yes and no. The psychology at play is a fascinating, and often hilarious, mix of evolutionary leftovers, cognitive biases, and the uniquely modern phenomenon of the digital friendship.

At the heart of it all lies the **parasocial relationship**, the imaginary friend we never knew we had. As discussed, our brains are wired for connection. When we spend hours a day with a streamer, our brains start to release all the same feel-good chemicals—oxytocin, dopamine—that they would in a real friendship. We feel like we know them. We’ve seen them at their best, their worst, and their most ridiculously sleep-deprived. So, when they recommend a game, it doesn’t feel like an advertisement; it feels like a recommendation from a trusted friend. It’s the digital equivalent of your buddy leaning over and saying, “Dude, you have to try this new craft beer.” Except your buddy is a millionaire with a sponsorship deal, and the craft beer is a free-to-play mobile game with more microtransactions than a Las Vegas casino.

This is where **social proof** comes in. When we see thousands of other people in the chat getting excited about a new game, our herd mentality kicks in. We think, “If all these people are into it, it must be good.” It’s the same reason we’ll stand in line for an hour for a cronut, even though we’re not entirely sure what a cronut is. The streamer, in this scenario, is the ultimate social proof. They are the alpha of the digital herd, and if they’re playing a game, it must be the cool thing to do. This is particularly effective in the gaming world, where trends can spread like wildfire. One day, everyone is playing a quiet, contemplative indie game about a sad fox, and the next, the entire internet is obsessed with a chaotic, physics-based goose simulator. All it takes is a few key influencers to start the fire.

But the whole system collapses without the crucial element of **authenticity**. The moment a streamer’s endorsement feels like a hostage video, the magic is gone. We, the discerning audience, have a finely tuned BS detector, and we can spot a fake a mile away. The most successful influencer campaigns are the ones that feel organic, the ones where the streamer seems to genuinely enjoy the game they’re playing. This is why so many sponsored streams fail. A streamer who has built their brand on playing hardcore, competitive shooters is not going to be able to convincingly sell a cutesy, casual farming simulator, no matter how much they’re getting paid. The audience will see right through it, and the backlash will be brutal. The streamer will be labeled a “sellout,” a traitor, a Judas in a gaming chair. And in the fickle world of online entertainment, that’s a fate worse than death.

Finally, there’s the **economic reality** of it all. Streamers, despite their seemingly idyllic lives of playing video games all day, are running a business. They have bills to pay, equipment to upgrade, and a seemingly endless supply of novelty-sized hats to purchase. Sponsorships are a necessary evil, a way to keep the lights on and the content flowing. And while we, the audience, may grumble and complain, deep down, we understand. We may not like it when our favorite streamer “sells out,” but we also don’t want them to go back to their old job at the gas station. So, we tolerate the sponsored streams, the branded overlays, and the endless parade of discount codes. We may even, in a moment of weakness, click on the link and download the game. Because, in the end, we’re not just buying a game; we’re supporting our “friend.” And what’s a few dollars between friends, especially when one of them has a much, much bigger bank account?

## Real-World Examples

The theoretical underpinnings of influencer marketing are all well and good, but the real entertainment lies in seeing them play out in the messy, chaotic real world. The history of gaming is littered with tales of influencer-driven triumphs and spectacular, cringe-inducing failures. Let’s examine a few of the most notable.

**The *Among Us* Phenomenon: A Perfect Storm of Influence**

In the summer of 2020, a little-known indie game called *Among Us* suddenly exploded in popularity, becoming a global phenomenon seemingly overnight. The game, which had been released two years prior to little fanfare, was suddenly being played by everyone, from your little cousin to your grandma. So, what happened? The answer, of course, is streamers. A handful of popular Twitch streamers started playing the game, and their massive audiences followed suit. The game’s simple, social, and endlessly meme-able gameplay was perfectly suited for streaming, and it quickly became the go-to game for group streams. The success of *Among Us* is a textbook example of the power of influencer marketing. It wasn’t a multi-million dollar advertising campaign that propelled the game to stardom; it was a handful of streamers who genuinely seemed to be having fun. The authenticity was palpable, the social proof was overwhelming, and the result was a cultural phenomenon that no amount of traditional marketing could have ever achieved.

**The Great Mixer Migration: When Selling Out Goes Wrong**

In 2019, the streaming world was rocked by a series of high-profile defections from Twitch to Microsoft’s now-defunct streaming platform, Mixer. The most notable of these was Tyler “Ninja” Blevins, who was reportedly paid a staggering $20-30 million to make the switch [5]. On the surface, it seemed like a brilliant move. Microsoft had secured the biggest name in streaming, and Ninja had secured a life-changing payday. But it didn’t take long for the cracks to appear. Ninja’s viewership on Mixer was a fraction of what it had been on Twitch, and the platform as a whole struggled to gain traction. In the end, Microsoft pulled the plug on Mixer in 2020, releasing Ninja from his golden handcuffs and sending him scurrying back to Twitch [6]. The Mixer experiment is a cautionary tale about the limits of influencer marketing. You can buy a streamer, but you can’t buy their community. The parasocial relationships that streamers build with their audience are not easily transferable, and the audience is not always willing to follow their favorite streamer to a new, unfamiliar platform, no matter how much money is involved.

**The Twitch Branded Content Fiasco: A Lesson in Tone-Deafness**

In June of 2023, Twitch, in its infinite wisdom, decided to roll out a new set of branded content guidelines that were, to put it mildly, not well-received. The new rules were seen as overly restrictive and a direct attack on the livelihoods of streamers. The backlash was immediate and ferocious. Streamers of all sizes, from the biggest names on the platform to the smallest up-and-comers, united in their condemnation of the new rules. The hashtag #TwitchSellsOut trended on Twitter, and the platform was flooded with angry messages and memes. To their credit, Twitch quickly backtracked, revoking the new guidelines and issuing a public apology [7]. The branded content fiasco is a powerful reminder of the delicate balance that platforms like Twitch must strike. They need to monetize their platform, but they can’t do so at the expense of their creators. The streamers are the lifeblood of the platform, and if they’re not happy, nobody’s happy. It’s a lesson that Twitch, and every other platform in the creator economy, would do well to remember.

## Discussion

So, what does this all mean for the future of gaming, marketing, and our increasingly fragile psyches? The rise of influencer marketing in gaming is more than just a new way to sell video games; it’s a reflection of a fundamental shift in how we consume media, form relationships, and make decisions. It’s a world where the lines between friend and entertainer, authenticity and advertising, are becoming increasingly blurred. And while it’s easy to be cynical, it’s also worth acknowledging the positive aspects of this new paradigm.

For all its potential pitfalls, influencer marketing has democratized the gaming industry in a way that was previously unimaginable. It has given a voice to smaller, independent developers who would have otherwise been drowned out by the marketing budgets of a few giant publishers. It has created a new generation of entertainers who have built careers on their own terms, outside the traditional gatekeepers of the media industry. And it has fostered a sense of community and connection for millions of people around the world, many of whom may feel isolated or alone in their offline lives. The parasocial relationships that we form with streamers may be one-sided, but that doesn’t mean they’re not real. They provide a sense of belonging, a shared experience, and a much-needed escape from the stresses of everyday life. And in a world that can often feel cold and isolating, that’s not something to be taken lightly.

Of course, there’s a dark side to all of this. The pressure to maintain an authentic, relatable persona while also paying the bills can take a serious toll on the mental health of streamers. The constant scrutiny, the fear of being labeled a “sellout,” and the ever-present threat of online harassment can be overwhelming. And for the audience, the constant exposure to a carefully curated, highly monetized version of reality can lead to a distorted sense of self, a feeling of inadequacy, and a perpetual, gnawing desire for the things we don’t have. It’s a high-wire act for everyone involved, and there’s no safety net.

## Conclusion

In the end, the phenomenon of influencer marketing in gaming is a microcosm of our modern, hyper-connected world. It’s a world of contradictions, where authenticity is a commodity, friendship is a brand, and the line between entertainment and advertising is so blurry it’s practically non-existent. We may roll our eyes when our favorite streamer starts shilling a new game, but we’re also the first to jump on the bandwagon when it’s a game we actually want to play. We may decry the evils of consumerism, but we’re also the ones who are eagerly awaiting the next big trend, the next big thing, the next digital messiah to lead us to the promised land of gaming nirvana.

So, the next time you see your favorite streamer “selling out,” take a moment to appreciate the beautiful, chaotic, and utterly absurd spectacle of it all. It’s a dance as old as time, the dance between art and commerce, authenticity and advertising. And in the grand, pixelated theater of the internet, it’s a dance that’s only going to get more interesting.
references:
[1] Horton, D., & Wohl, R. R. (1956). Mass communication and para-social interaction: Observations on intimacy at a distance. *Psychiatry*, *19*(3), 215-229.

[2] Healthy Gamer. (2021, July 15). *Parasocial Relationships on Twitch: A Psychiatrist's Perspective*. Healthy Gamer. https://www.healthygamer.gg/blog/parasocial-relationships-on-twitch-a-psychiatrists-perspective

[3] GameInfluencer. (2024, September 5). *The Psychology of Influencer Marketing: Understanding Consumer Behavior and Decision-Making in Gaming*. GameInfluencer. https://gameinfluencer.com/the-psychology-of-influencer-marketing-understanding-consumer-behavior-and-decision-making-in-gaming/

[4] Insight - Kellogg School of Management. (2024, May 14). *Video-Game Companies Are Spending Big on Sponsored Streams*. https://insight.kellogg.northwestern.edu/article/video-game-companies-are-spending-big-on-sponsored-streams

[5] GamesIndustry.biz. (2020, January 27). *Ninja was reportedly paid between $20m and $30m for Mixer exclusivity deal*. https://www.gamesindustry.biz/ninja-was-reportedly-paid-between-usd20m-and-usd30m-for-mixer-exclusivity-deal

[6] Video Games Chronicle. (2020, August 5). *Ninja has returned to Twitch, one year after signing a $50m Mixer deal*. https://www.videogameschronicle.com/news/ninja-has-returned-to-twitch-one-year-after-signing-a-50m-mixer-deal/

[7] TechCrunch. (2023, June 7). *Twitch backtracks on branded content changes after streamer backlash*. https://techcrunch.com/2023/06/07/twitch-backtracks-on-changes-to-branded-content-rules-after-streamer-backlash/
